text: Welcome to This is My Architecture New Zealand. I'm Andre and here is another Andre from Lancome.
Welcome to the show. Yeah, great to be here. Thanks. Can you tell me about Lancome and this solution?
Yeah, absolutely. So Lancome Technology, we're based here in Aotearoa, New Zealand. We're a technology provider,
so think software development, cloud infrastructure, that sort of thing. And today I'd like to share this architecture
we developed for a video conferencing solution that connects loss adjusters in the insurance industry
to clients that are making insurance claims. So basically what the application does is it connects
those loss adjustment agents via a remote video call that's facilitated by a custom web app to clients
that are connected just through their phone, sharing that video stream through their camera,
and it allows them to perform remote assessments of any damages relating to that claim.
Perfect. So you've got agents connecting to the clients. How do you actually connect the video? How
does it work? Yeah, absolutely. So we've got our two sets of users and they both connect to a video
platform called OpenVideo. It's an open source platform that is hosted on EC2. So OpenVideo facilitates those
video sessions and using a protocol called WebRTC. And it allows the agents to view those damages just
through the camera on the client's phone and assess those damages. So once the call is finished, a recording
of that is saved onto disk on the EC2 instance. So we have a little script that's just running here and
that will pick up those raw recording files and upload them to S3 where they can be later processed.
Now you have to save the video to S3 and you need to process them. So how do you do that?
So what we do is when the files are uploaded to S3, that triggers an event notification to SQS.
And those messages are polled by a service running on ECS Fargate. So what the service does is it takes
the raw files that are uploaded to S3 and processes them with a tool called FFMPIC. So that tool will
combine the audio and video streams of both parties and produce an output that is encoded to a more
consumable format called HLS or HTTP live streaming. And then those process files are uploaded back into
S3. So we've got our video files there. And they're saved to S3 for later review by the loss adjustment agents.
When I work with my insurance company, I usually call them during the business hours. How do you
handle the peaks and the highs and the lows? Yeah, so you know, of course, these companies have
times of peak traffic where there are many sessions ongoing and so lots of recordings to process.
So we've leveraged the scalability of Fargate to handle this. We have auto scaling policies that look at
the size of the SQS queue. So how many messages are waiting to be processed. So as that number increases
and we have more messages coming in, we are able to scale up the number of tasks automatically.
So that number of tasks increases and we're able to process those videos faster and reduce the amount
of time that the loss adjustment agents are waiting to review their videos.
And then can you then scale down if you need to as well?
Yeah, absolutely. So when the number of messages goes down or in the case of some small businesses
outside of business hours, it may even go all the way to zero and we can scale that all the way down
to zero as well. So that really saves on the costs there.
I love the flexibility for the big and small businesses. That's a good benefit of using Fargate.
I see it in the same time you're also using EC2. Can you walk us through what made you chose different
technologies? Absolutely. So, you know, we have leveraged Fargate where we can and the benefits
that it provides as being a serverless service. But in the case of OpenVideo, it supports an EC2-based
deployment. That's the option that we had to take. And so we were able to utilize this sort of
flexibility of EC2 platform to support that part of the architecture and deliver a complete solution.
I can see that being a lot of videos where all the agents are connecting. How do you make sure that you
manage the storage costs for the platform? Yeah. Yeah. So you can see we've got S3 Glacier here.
What we have is essentially some lifecycle policies that will allow us to reduce those long-term storage
costs. So there is a lot of data. When we're processing, we're actually outputting multiple
different resolutions to support dynamic playback. But those process files then can end up being, you know,
sort of up to two times larger than the raw files themselves. So there's a lot of data to manage,
but it's important that we retain those recordings in the long term for legal reasons. So to manage
those storage costs, after a certain amount of time has passed, we delete the process video files. And
then for the original raw recordings, we migrate those to S3 Glacier. And that really reduces those long-term
storage costs. And then in the event that we need to restore any of those archived recordings,
we can simply take that raw file and put it through the same process again and restore the video to its
original state. Now that's a perfect use case for Glacier when you can store the raw file and then
process them again if you need to. Well, thank you for sharing this architecture and particularly the
details that made you choose between Fargate and EC2. Yeah, no, thanks for having me. It's great to share
our experience with AWS. Thank you for watching. This is my architecture New Zealand.
